coredata.dc:title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots from cigarette filters and its application for fluorescence detection of Sudan I
coredata.dc:creator: Anmei, Su
Qingmei, Zhong
Yuye, Chen
Yilin, Wang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with quantum yield of 14% were successfully synthesized via a simple, low-cost, and green hydrothermal treatment using cigarette filters as carbon source for the first time. The obtained CQDs showed a strong emission at the wavelength of 465 nm, with an optimum excitation of 365 nm.Sudan I with maximum absorption wavelength at 477 nm could selectively quench the fluorescence of CQDs. Based on this principle, a fluorescence probe was developed for Sudan I determination. Furthermore, the quenching mechanism of the CQDs was elucidated. A linear relationship was found in the range of 2.40–104.0 μmol/L Sudan I with the detection limit (3σ/k) of 0.95 μmol/L. Satisfactory results were achieved when the method was submitted to the determination of Sudan I in food samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Determination
Sudan I
Fluorescence quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for determination of Sudan I
Effect of Sudan I on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi (2014GXNSFAA118328), the Opening Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Biorefinery (GXKLB-2) and the Dean Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Resource Processing and Process Intensification Technology (2016K003).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fluorescence spectra CQDs excited from 325 to 435 nm. Inset: normalized fluorescence spectra.
TEM and size distribution of CQDs.
Fluorescence spectra of CQDs in the presence of Sudan I. From top to bottom, the concentrations of Sudan I were 0.00, 2.40, 4.00, 8.00, 24.00, 40.00, 56.00, 72.00, 88.00, 104.00 μmol/L, respectively.
An overlap of absorption spectra of Sudan I and fluorescence spectra of CQDs.
Fluorescence responses of CQDs in the presence and absence of 22.00 μmol/L Sudan I at different pH values.
Fluorescence responses of CQDs in the presence of 22.00 μmol/L of Sudan I at different reaction time in pH 6.09.
Selection fluorescence response of CQDs toward 22.0 μmol/L Sudan I and 2200 μmol/L interfering substances.
The relationship between the fluorescence quenching and the concentration of Sudan I.
Determination results of Sudan I in food samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots from cigarette filters and its application for fluorescence detection of Sudan I

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Biorefinery, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Resource Processing and Process Intensification Technology, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Guangxi Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Resource Processing and Process Intensification Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with quantum yield of 14% were successfully synthesized via a simple, low-cost, and green hydrothermal treatment using cigarette filters as carbon source for the first time. The obtained CQDs showed a strong emission at the wavelength of 465 nm, with an optimum excitation of 365 nm.Sudan I with maximum absorption wavelength at 477 nm could selectively quench the fluorescence of CQDs. Based on this principle, a fluorescence probe was developed for Sudan I determination. Furthermore, the quenching mechanism of the CQDs was elucidated. A linear relationship was found in the range of 2.40–104.0 μmol/L Sudan I with the detection limit (3σ/k) of 0.95 μmol/L. Satisfactory results were achieved when the method was submitted to the determination of Sudan I in food samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Food colorants, either natural or synthetic, are a very important class of food additives. Compared with natural colorants, synthetic colorants are widely used in food industry because of their advantages such as good stability, excellent water solubility, strong coloring power and low cost. However, because synthetic colorantspose a potential risk to human health, the use of food colorants is strictly controlled by laws and regulations in all countries. To date, Various techniques including UV–Vis spectrometry [1,2], fluorescence emission spectrometry [3,4], thin-layer chromatography [5,6], high-performance liquid chromatography [7–9], capillary electrophoresis [10] and electrochemical method [11] have been established for the detection of synthetic colorants. Sudan I (1-phenylazo-2-naphthol) is a synthetic azo-colorant dye, which was previously used for coloring of various foods. Because of its high carcinogenic nature [12], Sudan I as a food additive is now strictly forbidden in many countries. Unfortunately, there are still many organizations that use Sudan I in their products due to its intense color and low cost. Furthermore, Sudan I still has been widely used in foods by some countries [13]. Therefore, monitoring the content of Sudan I in foods is of great significance to ensure food safety and consumer confidence.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a new kind of carbon nanomaterial, which exhibits strong fluorescence with tunable emission depending on the size and excitation wavelength. Compared with traditional semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes, CQDs are superior in terms of water solubility, photostability, toxicity and biocompatibility. Various approaches have been developed for the preparation of CQDs with desired possible applications in chemical analysis [14–16], biosensing [17,18], fluorescent imaging [19,20], etc. Among reported methods, the oxidation of graphite powder need a large amount of strong oxidants like concentrated sulfuric acids or concentrated nitric acids which are hazardous, and multi-step operations involved [21]. Recently, much effort has been made in the preparation of CQDs via high temperature or microwave-assisted hydrothermal carbonization of carbon precursors such as citric acid [22], ascorbic acid [23] and glucose [24]. So far, simple and green approaches for the large-scale preparation of CQDs from discarded materials are still challengeable.

In the present study, we report a facile and economical hydrothermal process for the preparation of CQDs by using discarded cigarette filters as carbon sources. The as-prepared CQDs have a size around 8 nm and exhibit excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence with a high quantum yield of 14% at the strongest emission wavelength of 465 nm. Most importantly, because the emission spectra of CQDs was effectively overlapped with the absorption spectra of carmine, significant fluorescence quenching of the CQDs was found upon addition of different concentrations of Sudan I. Thus, the CQDs can serve as a fluorescent probe for detection of Sudan I with high sensitivity and selectivity. To the best of our knowledge, there are no previous reports on the preparation of CQDs using discarded cigarette filter. And the use of CQDs as fluorescence probe for determination of Sudan I has also not been reported.

The UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were obtained, respectively, with a UV-4802 (Unico, Shanghai, China) and a RF-5301PC spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI Company, USA) image was taken at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. FTIR spectra were recorded on Nicolet iS50 FTR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Co., Ltd. Madison, WI, USA). Size distribution of the CQDs was observed using a Zeta sizer Nano ZS instrument (Malvern, UK).

Sudan I was obtained from Adamas Reagent (Shanghai, Co., Ltd., China). The Britton-Robison (B-R) buffer solution with different pH values were prepared by adding various amounts of 0.2 mol/L sodium hydroxide solution into 0.04 mol/L mixture of phosphoric acid, ethylic acid and boric acid. All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

CQDs were prepared via a hydrothermal method with discarded cigarette filters as carbon source. In a typical procedure, the cigarette filters was first washed with water several times and then dried in oven at 60 °C till reaching weight no further change. 0.4 g pretreated cigarette filters were mixed with 40 mL of doubly deionized water (DDW) and then the mixture was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 200 °C for a period of 6 h in an oven. After the reaction, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The solution was filtered through 0.45 μm membrane filter and diluted to 200 mL for further use.

Chilli powder, chilli sauce and tomato sauce were selected as test samples because Sudan I may be added as a colorant into them. All samples were obtained from a local supermarket in Nanning, China. The samples were pretreated according to the method from a literature [25], with some modification. A sample of 6.0 g added with a certain amount of Sudan I (0, 2.5, 12.5, or 25.0 mg, respectively) were mixed with 50 mL of ethanol. After being sonicated for 20 min, the mixture was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was collected and filtered through a 0.45 μm polytetrafluoroethylene filter membrane. The filtrate was diluted with ethanol to 100 mL, and 1.0 mL aliquot of the resulting solution was taken out for analysis according to the determination procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for determination of Sudan I
ce:section.ce:para: The determination of Sudan I was performed at room temperature. In a typical assay, B-R buffer (pH = 6.09, 5.0 mL), CQDs solution (1.0 mL), and various amounts of Sudan I standard solutions (800 μmol/L, ethanol as solvent) were sequentially added to a series of 10 mL colorimetric tubes. Then the mixture solution was diluted with ethanol to the mark and thoroughly mixed. After 30 min, the fluorescence spectra was measured at λex/λem = 365/465 nm, the slit widths of excitation and emission were set to 3 nm and 5 nm.

The CQDs possess wavelength dependent fluorescent emission properties. Under different excitation wave-length, the corresponding surface state emissive trap will be dominant, giving excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 1 , with the increase of excitation wavelength from 325 to 435 nm, emission peaks are red-shifted from 437 to 500 nm. This is believed that the giant red-edge effect is the reason for the strong dependence of the emission peak position upon the excitation wavelength [26]. And the emission intensity reached a maximum when excited at 365 nm.Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of as-prepared CQDs is measured by comparing the wavelength integrated intensity of testing sample to that of Rhodamine6G (QY = 95%). According to the calculation method from literature [27], the QY of CQDs is estimated to be 14%, which is comparable to those prepared using other carbon precursors [28,29]. Furthermore, the obtained CQDs were very stable in the dark under ambient conditions. After kept for 90 days, the fluorescence spectra almost had no change (Fig. S1), and no obvious precipitations were observed. As determined by dynamic light scattering (Fig. S2), the size distributions of CQDs before and after being aged were found within the range 140–320 nm, and 140–400 nm, respectively.

The functional groups of CQDs were demonstrated by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2 , the peak at 3412, 1727, and 1252 cm−1 are ascribed to O-H, C=O and C-O-C stretching vibration, respectively, and the peak at about 2907 and 1666 cm−1 are due to C-Hand C=C stretching vibrations. The above FTIR spectra results shown that the surface of the obtained CQDs were filled with hydrophilic groups (-OH and -COOH), which insured CQDs were readily water-dispersible and excellent fluorescence.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were carried out to study the size and morphology of CQDs. As can be seen in Fig. 3 , the obtained particles are spherical in shape and show good homogeneity in the particle size distribution. The statistics shows that the diameters of CQDs are mainly distributed in the range of 2.4–13.6 nm and average diameter is calculated to be 8.0 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Sudan I on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of Sudan I on the fluorescence spectra of CQDs has been investigated. As seen in Fig. 4 , it is evident that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs decreases as the Sudan I concentration increases. The researches [30–32] disclosed that the fluorescence quenching can be carried out through charge transfer or energy transfer. The former is caused by the charge transfer between the quencher and the excited molecules of the fluorophore. And the latter is resulted from the absorption of fluorescence by the quencher. Energy transfer quenching can take place under the following conditions: (1) the fluorescence emission spectra of the donor and the UV absorbance spectra of the acceptor have sufficient overlap; (2) the distance between the donor and the acceptor is very short, typically within 10 nm. In this work, the position of the fluorescence maximum of CQDs is located at 465 nm upon excitation of 365 nm, while the maximum absorption wavelength of Sudan I is located at 477 nm, and the spectra of the two are overlapped effectively (Fig. 5 ). Moreover, the supramolecular interactions such as C−H⋯O hydrogen bond type and π-stacking between CQDs and Sudan I which can shorten the distance between each other [33] (Fig. S2). Therefore, we believe that the quenching effect of Sudan I on the fluorescence of CQDs is attributed to an energy transfer. According to the Forster non-radiation energy transfer theory [34], we can get the critical distance R 0 = 4.1 nm. And the maximum energy transfer efficiency was calculated as 59.8% (See supplementary material).
Considering the quenching of fluorescence observed in the presence of Sudan I, the possibility of developing a sensitive method for determination of Sudan I has been investigated.

The influence of pH in arrange between 2.09 and 11.92 was studied in order to select the best acidity for the determination of Sudan I with CQDs. The results in Fig. 6 showed that pH value is effective on the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs solution in absence and presence of Sudan I. The emission of CQDs solution in both absence and presence of Sudan I increased with the increasing of pH from 2.09 to 6.09, and then remained relatively stable in a pH range from 6.09 to 11.92, whereas the relative fluorescence intensity values (F 0/F) almost had no change in the whole pH range. Therefore, pH = 6.09 was chosen as an optimum solution pH for further experiments. Then at pH = 6.09, effects of different buffer systems (such as Tris-HCl, Britton-Robinson (B-R) and phosphate salt) on the F 0/F were studied. Based on the results, B-R buffer solution was the most efficient among the above-mentioned buffer systems.

The effect of incubation times on fluorescence intensity value was also examined. After all reagents had been added, fluorescence measurement was performed every 5 min. As observed in Fig. 7 , the fluorescence intensity value reached the maximum after 30 min and kept constant in 60 min. Thus, fluorescence intensity was measured after the system was 30 min later.

To evaluate the specificity of this method, the influences of some potential interfering substances such as sugars (lactose, maltose, fructose, glucose), amino acids (dl-alanine, l-lysine, l-aspartic acid, l-threonine, l-phenylalanine, glycine, l-tryptophan, l-serine, l-valine, l-arginine), vitamins (vitamin C, vitamin B1, vitamin B6), salts (KCl, MgSO4, CaCl2, MnCl2, CuCl2, NiSO4, NH4Cl, Zn(CH3COO)2, sodium benzoate, potassium sorbate, calgon) on the determination of Sudan I was individually studied under the above-optimized conditions. As shown in Fig. 8 , the results were obtained by mixing 22.0 μmol/L of Sudan I with CQDs alone and mixing 22.0 μmol/L of Sudan I and 2200 μmol/L of potential interfering substances with the CQDs respectively. The results shown that the potentially interfering had minor or no interference on the fluorescence quenching response to Sudan I except for Cu2+, Ni2+ and vitamin C. However, the contents of these substances in real food samples are often very low. Therefore, the method showed a good selectivity for the detection of Sudan I.

The calibration curve for determination of Sudan I was presented in Fig. 9 . It was found that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs quenched by Sudan I in a relationship that was best described by the well-known Stern-Volmer equation: F 0/F = K sv [Q] + 1, where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of CQDs in the absence and presence of Sudan I; K sv is a Stern-Volmer quenching constant; [Q] denotes the concentration of quencher (Sudan I). The linear regression equation was F 0/F = 0.0134C + 0.9376, where C was the concentration of Sudan I, the unit was μmol/L. The linear range was from 2.40 to 104.0 μmol/L with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.991. The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.95 μmol/L based on 3σ/k. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for five replicate determinations was 1.4% for 22.0 μmol/L Sudan I, suggesting one of the most sensitive methods for determination of Sudan I.

The developed method has been applied to the determination of Sudan I in chill powder, chill sauce and tomato sauce samples. According to the procedures described in section 2.4, 1.0 mL of pretreated samples was determined. As shown in Table 1 , no Sudan I was detected in these samples. Known concentrations of Sudan I were added to the detected samples and determined according to the procedures, the recovery results showed in Table 1 were in the range from 95.8 to 105.9%. Therefore, the proposed method is reliable and can be applied for Sudan I determination in real samples.

In this study, we have developed a green and simple hydrothermal method for the preparation of CQDs from cigarette filter for the first time. The as-prepared CQDs were used as a novel fluorescence probe for the highly sensitive and selective detection of Sudan I. Under the optimum conditions, calibration curve was linear in the range of 2.40–104.0 μmol/L with the correlation coefficient of 0.991, the detection limit of Sudan I was 0.95 μmol/L. Furthermore, the feasibility of the method has been proven by the determination of Sudan I in real samples with satisfactory results. To the best our knowledge, this is the first report which uses CQDs as fluorescence probe for determination of Sudan I.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi (2014GXNSFAA118328), the Opening Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Biorefinery (GXKLB-2) and the Dean Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Resource Processing and Process Intensification Technology (2016K003).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2018.03.024.
